can either go spend the next three to six...

find someone that's already publishing really good content in that space.

then they will come to me and be, and, you know, three months in, I would look and we're spending, you know, 20, 30 grand a month and writing fees for writers and whatnot.

Right.

guys.

So the way we set up the team, right, now to give you kind of that additional context was, okay, so then at DeepGram, for the first time ever, when I was building the stuff, I had a dev advocate, Jose, you know, very junior guy, was super smart, like, gritty person.

But then every single day, run a daily audit on the site, and just go fix it if you can.
And if you can't, go, like, send it to the web team and make sure it gets fixed, it's getting top of it.

And after we 24x our traffic, what I realized was like, hey, even if I have the best workflows on the planet, fully customized to my company, if I didn't have the right strategy, it would fail.

So the right strategy to grow organically.
So think of it as, right, like in Deepgram's case, we tried a lot of things and the strategy wasn't just like one piece of the strategy.
It was like, hey, we're going to do an AI apps catalog, not because people searching for AI apps are our audience, but because the people that own those apps want distribution.
And so if we own the audience, then those people that are building the app are going to want to be featured here.
And then we're going to build a startup program and a newsletter to feature these apps here and talk about different topics.
And then the blog that goes way deeper into technical topics.
And they're all going to connect to each other.
So that was the bigger strategy, if you will.
And so it's more like a growth strategy as well as how do we convert this audience and things like that.
And then the other part was there's just grunt work that needs to happen.
Yes, there's editorial work as well.
That's important.
But in addition to that, there's also just  that's just time consuming that is important.

don't want to go Right.

Yeah, and I think just maybe the last thing, Dennis, is in that process, we started to essentially realize that the strategy piece was really critical, but then also the content managers just weren't the right level and persona to be client-facing.

Reliant on content managers as like doing a lot of the heavy lifting on kind of things on the day-to-day, but be behind the scenes, right?
And that's when we realized as well, it's like, obviously, like we also need a director layer that is truly the strategy and it's people that clients want to hire.

Or if they could, it would be really hard to coach somebody, right?
Like really good.

or try to onboard new people on the team and whatnot.
